user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.5,00,000/- being a hand loan given by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant. The loan was repayable on demand or within 6 months. The Plaintiff demanded repayment on 15.09.2024 through legal notice dated 20.09.2024 but the Defendant has failed to repay. Plead under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for damages and recovery. Claim interest at 12% per annum from date of loan. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation, court fee and verification. Draft suitable for filing before the Civil Judge Senior Division.

facts.summary: Suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000/- as a hand loan given on 10.03.2024, evidenced by a promissory note. Defendant failed to repay despite demand via legal notice dated 20.09.2024. Plaintiff claims interest at 12% p.a. and damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act.

payment of one or more instalments the whole amount shall be payable, there was a conflict of decisions as to 
whether a suit for the unpaid instalment or instalments would bar a suit for the balance. The Allahabad High Court

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the advance amount was transferred from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor returned the advance amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

examples.bad: Under Section 65, the Defendant is bound to restore the amount.
examples.good: Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, the business transaction between the parties has resulted in a total failure of consideration inasmuch as the Defendant received Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- but provided no benefit whatsoever to the Plaintiff.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations under the business transaction despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim refund of the advance amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding refund, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is a person of sound mind and is sui juris and competent to sue in this Court. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is a person of sound mind and is sui juris.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text: The Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}} and, in the alternative, carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_BUSINESS_LOCATION}} within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court, the Civil Judge Senior Division at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff advanced a hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on 10.03.2024; payment was made by the Plaintiff at {{PAYMENT_LOCATION}} on that date and the loan was evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant on 10.03.2024 at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}}. The cause of action arose on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff issued a demand for repayment, and the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay after the demand dated 20.09.2024. Accordingly, the suit is appropriately filed before the Civil Judge Senior Division at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks Rs. 5,00,000 as principal and claims interest at 12% per annum, so the total value of relief sought is within the monetary limits of this Court.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. On {{DATE}}, at {{PLACE_NAME}}, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} advanced to the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} a hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000, the amount being evidenced by the Defendant executing a promissory note in favour of the Plaintiff; a true copy of the promissory note is annexed as Annexure A.

2. The promissory note executed on {{DATE}} provided that the loan amount of Rs. 5,00,000 was repayable on demand or within six months from the date of execution and that interest would accrue on the principal at 12% per annum; the terms are reflected in Annexure A.

3. By the terms of the loan, the Defendant undertook to repay the principal and interest thereon within the period set out in the promissory note and the Plaintiff reserved the right to demand payment at any time during the currency of the loan subject to the six-month limit; the timeframe for performance under the note would culminate on {{DATE}} if not paid earlier.

4. The Plaintiff disbursed Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on {{DATE}}, as a hand loan; the transaction was recorded by the promissory note and is evidenced by Annexure A.

5. The due date passed with no payment from the Defendant; on {{DATE}} the Plaintiff demanded repayment by issuing a legal notice dated {{DATE}}; despite the demand and service of the legal notice, the Defendant did not pay any amount due.

6. Following the default, the Plaintiff issued a formal legal notice dated {{DATE}} to the Defendant demanding repayment; the notice is annexed as Annexure C; there has been no compliance or payment by the Defendant as of the date of filing; these events form the basis of the Plaintiff’s suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000 together with interest at 12% per annum and damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: The legal basis for the relief sought is Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which creates a statutory obligation to compensate a party for loss or damage caused by a breach of contract, and requires that, in estimating the loss arising from such breach, the means existing of remedying the inconvenience caused by non-performance must be taken into account. The Plaintiff, Plaintiff_name, advanced Rs. 5,00,000 to Defendant_name on 10.03.2024 at {{LOAN_LOCATION}}, evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant at {{PROMISSORY_NOTE_EXECUTION_PLACE}}, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months. The Plaintiff demanded repayment on 15.09.2024 and further served a legal notice dated 20.09.2024, but the Defendant has failed to repay. The failure to honor the on-demand loan constitutes a breach of the contract evidenced by the promissory note, triggering the Plaintiff’s right to damages under Section 73. The damages claimed comprise the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and interest at 12% per annum from the date of loan (10.03.2024) until realization, reflecting the loss occasioned by the breach and the proper restitution of money due under the contract. The Plaintiff pleads failure of consideration in the sense that the Defendant accepted the loan on the terms of repayment on demand or within six months and has not performed his obligation despite demand, thereby rendering the plaintiff entitled to restitution of the money advanced and the interest accrued thereon. The remedy sought is restitution of the Rs. 5,00,000 plus interest, grounded in the breach of the loan contract evidenced by the promissory note and reinforced by the service of the legal notice. Limitation is governed by Article 19 of the Limitation Act, 1963, accrual being on the date the loan was made, i.e., 10.03.2024, and the suit is within the three-year period. For purposes of court-fee and valuation, the suit is valued at Rs. 5,00,000 principal with the claim for interest admissible under Section 73; The Plaintiff submits that no counter-claim has been pleaded by Defendant and no adjustment clause exists; The promissory note itself constitutes part performance of the contract, and the demand made and legal notice served establish the breach. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff prays for such damages as are permissible under Section 73, including interest at 12% per annum from the date of loan until realization, and costs. Verification: I, Plaintiff_name, say that the contents of this section are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief, and that I have not suppressed any material fact.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/-, advanced by the Plaintiff on 10.03.2024 and evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant in favor of the Plaintiff, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months, and by executing and delivering the promissory note the Defendant undertook an unconditional obligation to repay and the Plaintiff thereby acquired a legal right to sue upon default. The cause further accrued on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff formally demanded repayment and on 20.09.2024 when a legal notice was served upon the Defendant, who again failed, neglected and refused to repay despite service of the notice; notwithstanding these demands, the Defendant remains in default and the Plaintiff is entitled to damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for the breach of the promissory obligation and to recover the principal sum along with interest at 12% per annum from the date of the loan. The cause is continuing in nature because the Defendant continues to retain the entire principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and the interest that has accrued thereon, despite the Plaintiff’s demand and service of legal notice, to date, thereby constituting ongoing wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s money and justifying recovery of the debt with interest and damages as may be determined by the Court.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: LIMITATION: The present suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000/- is governed by Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years for suits to recover money payable for money lent. The accrual rule under Article 19 is that the right to sue for money lent arises when the money becomes due; in the instant case the hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000/- was repayable on demand or within six months from 10.03.2024, and the cause of action first accrued on 15.09.2024 when the plaintiff demanded repayment and the defendant defaulted thereafter. Consequently, the limitation period runs from 15.09.2024 and would expire on {{DATE}}, rendering the suit within time if filed on or before that date. Accrual, as applicable under the statute, is thus “when the loan is made,” and in this matter the cause of action for repayment anchored to 15.09.2024 confirms the within-time status of the suit.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text:
VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued at Rs. 5,00,000, representing the principal sum due under the promissory note executed by the Defendant on {{DATE}} in favour of the Plaintiff for a hand loan. The valuation reflects the amount claimed as due on the date of filing and forms the basis for computation of court fee and pecuniary jurisdiction. The court fee payable on the plaint is calculated under the applicable state Court Fees Act and amounts to {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff contends that Rs. 5,00,000 was advanced to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 under a promissory note executed by the Defendant, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months. The Defendant defaulted on repayment on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff issued a demand for repayment on 15.09.2024 followed by a legal notice dated 20.09.2024 calling upon the Defendant to repay the principal. From the date of default until the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit interest on the outstanding amount at the rate of 12% per annum. The rate is justified as appropriate compensation for the time value of money in a commercial transaction and as a remedy for wrongful retention of funds where no higher rate is contractually specified in the promissory note; the prevailing practice in unsecured lending and the parties’ commercial understanding support a rate in this range, and the rate aligns with the contract’s lack of an express higher rate. This interest claim forms part of the Plaintiff’s broader claim for damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for breach and recovery of money.

Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the principal amount at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of filing of the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} up to decree or final order, by operation of Section 34 CPC, which permits the Court to award interest on money due or damages during the course of litigation at such rate as it deems fit. The justification mirrors the pre-suit rationale: the loan was a commercial advance, the Defendant has retained the funds during the litigation, and the rate reflects the time value of money and the risk of non-payment in the context of a promissory-note obligation. The objective is to secure fair compensation for mirroring the consequences of continued non-payment during the suit.

Future interest: Post-decree, the Plaintiff seeks interest on the decretal amount at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of decree until realization. The appropriate legal basis for post-decree interest is the Court’s discretion under Section 34 CPC to award interest on money decreed until realisation, and in the present case the rate is justified by the commercial nature of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention, and the need to provide full compensation for the breach of contract under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act. The requested rate serves as a consistent measure of the time value of money and acts as a deterrent against future delays in payment.

In sum, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit, pendente lite, and future interest at the rate described above to reflect the commercial character of the loan, the Defendant’s failure to repay despite demand, and the necessity of protecting the Plaintiff’s monetary rights through the litigation process, consistent with the promissory note evidence and the breach of contract claim.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, invoking the cause of action arising from the hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000 advanced on {{DATE}} and evidenced by the promissory note executed by the Defendant, prays for the following reliefs. (a) A decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff Rs. 5,00,000 as the principal amount lent on {{DATE}}, evidenced by Promissory Note executed by the Defendant, together with interest and costs. (b) Pre-suit interest on the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 at the rate of 12% per annum for the period from {{DATE}} to {{DATE}}, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PRESUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}; (c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretable amount from the date of filing of the suit until the date of decree, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}; (d) Future interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization; (e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice, quantified as Rs. {{COURT_FEE}} for court fee and Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} for pre-litigation costs, and such further sums as may be directed by the Court; (f) Any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case; (g) General relief that the Court may grant such other and further reliefs as may be appropriate in law and equity in view of the facts and circumstances of the case.

In sum, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit, pendente lite, and future interest at the rate described above to reflect the commercial character of the loan, the Defendant’s failure to repay despite demand, and the necessity of protecting the Plaintiff’s monetary rights through the litigation process, consistent with the promissory note evidence and the breach of contract claim.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff, invoking the cause of action arising from the hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000 advanced on {{DATE}} and evidenced by the promissory note executed by the Defendant, prays for the following reliefs. (a) A decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff Rs. 5,00,000 as the principal amount lent on {{DATE}}, evidenced by Promissory Note executed by the Defendant, together with interest and costs. (b) Pre-suit interest on the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 at the rate of 12% per annum for the period from {{DATE}} to {{DATE}}, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PRESUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}; (c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretable amount from the date of filing of the suit until the date of decree, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}; (d) Future interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization; (e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice, quantified as Rs. {{COURT_FEE}} for court fee and Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} for pre-litigation costs, and such further sums as may be directed by the Court; (f) Any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case; (g) General relief that the Court may grant such other and further reliefs as may be appropriate in law and equity in view of the facts and circumstances of the case.

draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is a person of sound mind and is sui juris and competent to sue in this Court. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is a person of sound mind and is sui juris.


JURISDICTION

The Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}} and, in the alternative, carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_BUSINESS_LOCATION}} within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court, the Civil Judge Senior Division at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff advanced a hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on 10.03.2024; payment was made by the Plaintiff at {{PAYMENT_LOCATION}} on that date and the loan was evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant on 10.03.2024 at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}}. The cause of action arose on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff issued a demand for repayment, and the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay after the demand dated 20.09.2024. Accordingly, the suit is appropriately filed before the Civil Judge Senior Division at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks Rs. 5,00,000 as principal and claims interest at 12% per annum, so the total value of relief sought is within the monetary limits of this Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. On {{DATE}}, at {{PLACE_NAME}}, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} advanced to the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} a hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000, the amount being evidenced by the Defendant executing a promissory note in favour of the Plaintiff; a true copy of the promissory note is annexed as Annexure A.

2. The promissory note executed on {{DATE}} provided that the loan amount of Rs. 5,00,000 was repayable on demand or within six months from the date of execution and that interest would accrue on the principal at 12% per annum; the terms are reflected in Annexure A.

3. By the terms of the loan, the Defendant undertook to repay the principal and interest thereon within the period set out in the promissory note and the Plaintiff reserved the right to demand payment at any time during the currency of the loan subject to the six-month limit; the timeframe for performance under the note would culminate on {{DATE}} if not paid earlier.

4. The Plaintiff disbursed Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on {{DATE}}, as a hand loan; the transaction was recorded by the promissory note and is evidenced by Annexure A.

5. The due date passed with no payment from the Defendant; on {{DATE}} the Plaintiff demanded repayment by issuing a legal notice dated {{DATE}}; despite the demand and service of the legal notice, the Defendant did not pay any amount due.

6. Following the default, the Plaintiff issued a formal legal notice dated {{DATE}} to the Defendant demanding repayment; the notice is annexed as Annexure C; there has been no compliance or payment by the Defendant as of the date of filing; these events form the basis of the Plaintiff’s suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000 together with interest at 12% per annum and damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

The legal basis for the relief sought is Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which creates a statutory obligation to compensate a party for loss or damage caused by a breach of contract, and requires that, in estimating the loss arising from such breach, the means existing of remedying the inconvenience caused by non-performance must be taken into account. The Plaintiff, Plaintiff_name, advanced Rs. 5,00,000 to Defendant_name on 10.03.2024 at {{LOAN_LOCATION}}, evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant at {{PROMISSORY_NOTE_EXECUTION_PLACE}}, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months. The Plaintiff demanded repayment on 15.09.2024 and further served a legal notice dated 20.09.2024, but the Defendant has failed to repay. The failure to honor the on-demand loan constitutes a breach of the contract evidenced by the promissory note, triggering the Plaintiff’s right to damages under Section 73. The damages claimed comprise the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and interest at 12% per annum from the date of loan (10.03.2024) until realization, reflecting the loss occasioned by the breach and the proper restitution of money due under the contract. The Plaintiff pleads failure of consideration in the sense that the Defendant accepted the loan on the terms of repayment on demand or within six months and has not performed his obligation despite demand, thereby rendering the plaintiff entitled to restitution of the money advanced and the interest accrued thereon. The remedy sought is restitution of the Rs. 5,00,000 plus interest, grounded in the breach of the loan contract evidenced by the promissory note and reinforced by the service of the legal notice. Limitation is governed by Article 19 of the Limitation Act, 1963, accrual being on the date the loan was made, i.e., 10.03.2024, and the suit is within the three-year period. For purposes of court-fee and valuation, the suit is valued at Rs. 5,00,000 principal with the claim for interest admissible under Section 73; The Plaintiff submits that no counter-claim has been pleaded by Defendant and no adjustment clause exists; The promissory note itself constitutes part performance of the contract, and the demand made and legal notice served establish the breach. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff prays for such damages as are permissible under Section 73, including interest at 12% per annum from the date of loan until realization, and costs. Verification: I, Plaintiff_name, say that the contents of this section are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief, and that I have not suppressed any material fact.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/-, advanced by the Plaintiff on 10.03.2024 and evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant in favor of the Plaintiff, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months, and by executing and delivering the promissory note the Defendant undertook an unconditional obligation to repay and the Plaintiff thereby acquired a legal right to sue upon default. The cause further accrued on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff formally demanded repayment and on 20.09.2024 when a legal notice was served upon the Defendant, who again failed, neglected and refused to repay despite service of the notice; notwithstanding these demands, the Defendant remains in default and the Plaintiff is entitled to damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for the breach of the promissory obligation and to recover the principal sum along with interest at 12% per annum from the date of the loan. The cause is continuing in nature because the Defendant continues to retain the entire principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and the interest that has accrued thereon, despite the Plaintiff’s demand and service of legal notice, to date, thereby constituting ongoing wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s money and justifying recovery of the debt with interest and damages as may be determined by the Court.


LIMITATION

LIMITATION: The present suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000/- is governed by Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years for suits to recover money payable for money lent. The accrual rule under Article 19 is that the right to sue for money lent arises when the money becomes due; in the instant case the hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000/- was repayable on demand or within six months from 10.03.2024, and the cause of action first accrued on 15.09.2024 when the plaintiff demanded repayment and the defendant defaulted thereafter. Consequently, the limitation period runs from 15.09.2024 and would expire on {{DATE}}, rendering the suit within time if filed on or before that date. Accrual, as applicable under the statute, is thus “when the loan is made,” and in this matter the cause of action for repayment anchored to 15.09.2024 confirms the within-time status of the suit.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued at Rs. 5,00,000, representing the principal sum due under the promissory note executed by the Defendant on {{DATE}} in favour of the Plaintiff for a hand loan. The valuation reflects the amount claimed as due on the date of filing and forms the basis for computation of court fee and pecuniary jurisdiction. The court fee payable on the plaint is calculated under the applicable state Court Fees Act and amounts to {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff contends that Rs. 5,00,000 was advanced to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 under a promissory note executed by the Defendant, the loan being repayable on demand or within six months. The Defendant defaulted on repayment on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff issued a demand for repayment on 15.09.2024 followed by a legal notice dated 20.09.2024 calling upon the Defendant to repay the principal. From the date of default until the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit interest on the outstanding amount at the rate of 12% per annum. The rate is justified as appropriate compensation for the time value of money in a commercial transaction and as a remedy for wrongful retention of funds where no higher rate is contractually specified in the promissory note; the prevailing practice in unsecured lending and the parties’ commercial understanding support a rate in this range, and the rate aligns with the contract’s lack of an express higher rate. This interest claim forms part of the Plaintiff’s broader claim for damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for breach and recovery of money.

Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the principal amount at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of filing of the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} up to decree or final order, by operation of Section 34 CPC, which permits the Court to award interest on money due or damages during the course of litigation at such rate as it deems fit. The justification mirrors the pre-suit rationale: the loan was a commercial advance, the Defendant has retained the funds during the litigation, and the rate reflects the time value of money and the risk of non-payment in the context of a promissory-note obligation. The objective is to secure fair compensation for mirroring the consequences of continued non-payment during the suit.

Future interest: Post-decree, the Plaintiff seeks interest on the decretal amount at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of decree until realization. The appropriate legal basis for post-decree interest is the Court’s discretion under Section 34 CPC to award interest on money decreed until realisation, and in the present case the rate is justified by the commercial nature of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention, and the need to provide full compensation for the breach of contract under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act. The requested rate serves as a consistent measure of the time value of money and acts as a deterrent against future delays in payment.

In sum, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit, pendente lite, and future interest at the rate described above to reflect the commercial character of the loan, the Defendant’s failure to repay despite demand, and the necessity of protecting the Plaintiff’s monetary rights through the litigation process, consistent with the promissory note evidence and the breach of contract claim.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff, invoking the cause of action arising from the hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000 advanced on {{DATE}} and evidenced by the promissory note executed by the Defendant, prays for the following reliefs. (a) A decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff Rs. 5,00,000 as the principal amount lent on {{DATE}}, evidenced by Promissory Note executed by the Defendant, together with interest and costs. (b) Pre-suit interest on the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 at the rate of 12% per annum for the period from {{DATE}} to {{DATE}}, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PRESUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}; (c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretable amount from the date of filing of the suit until the date of decree, the amount of such interest being Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}; (d) Future interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization; (e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice, quantified as Rs. {{COURT_FEE}} for court fee and Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} for pre-litigation costs, and such further sums as may be directed by the Court; (f) Any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case; (g) General relief that the Court may grant such other and further reliefs as may be appropriate in law and equity in view of the facts and circumstances of the case.
